name: Ise Grand Shrine
sacredSignificance:

longDescription:
Ise, Mie Prefecture, Japan Ise Grand Shrine (Ise Jingu) is the most sacred shrine in Japan and the spiritual heart of Shinto,
                Japan's indigenous religion. Set within a vast ancient forest of cryptomeria cedars covering over
                5,500 hectares, the shrine complex comprises 125 shrines centered on the Inner Shrine (Naiku), dedicated
                to Amaterasu Omikami, the Sun Goddess and divine ancestor of the Imperial family, and the Outer Shrine
                (Geku), dedicated to Toyouke Omikami, goddess of agriculture and industry. Founded nearly 2,000 years
                ago according to tradition, Ise Jingu embodies the essence of Shinto's reverence for nature, purity,
                and the continuity of Japanese culture across millennia.
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.

geography: Often features distinctive natural formations associated with sacred narratives

keyMyths.title: The Sun Goddess Amaterasu's Retreat to Ama-no-Iwato
keyMyths.description: Amaterasu, the sun goddess, angered by her brother Susano-o's misdeeds, retreats into a cave called Ama-no-Iwato, plunging the world into darkness. This myth is central to the Ise Grand Shrine as it enshrines Amaterasu, and the ritualistic reenactments aim to prevent such cosmic disruptions.

keyMyths.title: The Sacred Mirror, Yata no Kagami
keyMyths.description: The Yata no Kagami, one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, is enshrined at Ise Grand Shrine. It was used to lure Amaterasu out of the cave, reflecting her radiant light and signifying the restoration of order and light to the world.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Inner Shrine (Naiku)
keyMyths.description: According to tradition, Princess Yamatohime-no-mikoto, daughter of Emperor Suinin, wandered the land seeking a permanent home for Amaterasu. Guided by divine will, she established the Inner Shrine at Ise, dedicating it to the worship of the sun goddess.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Outer Shrine (Geku)
keyMyths.description: Several centuries after the Naiku was established, the deity Toyouke-Ōmikami, responsible for food, clothing, and shelter, appeared in a dream of Emperor Yuryaku. The emperor, following the divine guidance, established the Geku to provide for Amaterasu's needs.

keyMyths.title: The Shikinen Sengu Ritual
keyMyths.description: Every twenty years, the Ise Grand Shrine is completely rebuilt in a ritual called Shikinen Sengu. This practice ensures the purity and renewal of the shrine, symbolizing the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, and maintaining the connection between the human and divine realms.

sources.name: Kojiki
sources.description: The Records of Ancient Matters, an early Japanese chronicle of myths, legends, songs, genealogies, oral traditions and semi-historical accounts.
sources.title: Kojiki

sources.name: Nihon Shoki
sources.description: The Chronicles of Japan, the second oldest book of classical Japanese history, commissioned by Emperor Tenmu.
sources.title: Nihon Shoki

sources.name: Ise Daijingū Gishikichō
sources.description: Records of the Rituals of Ise Grand Shrine, detailing the ceremonies and practices associated with the shrine.
sources.title: Ise Daijingū Gishikichō

sources.name: Watarai Shintō texts
sources.description: A body of Shinto texts associated with the Watarai clan, who traditionally served at the Outer Shrine (Geku) of Ise Grand Shrine.
sources.title: Watarai Shintō texts

sources.name: Jingu Shikinen Sengu Yōroku
sources.description: A detailed record documenting the Shikinen Sengu ritual, including its history, procedures, and significance.
sources.title: Jingu Shikinen Sengu Yōroku
